subtopic: International Big Cat Alliance and Small Cats
title: International Big Cat Alliance (IBCA)
focus: Genesis, structure, membership, and conservation targets of the IBCA.
rationale: IBCA is a major recent international initiative led by India, highly relevant for UPSC Prelims regarding its target species, membership, and structure.
sectionExcerpt: Launched by India in 2023 to commemorate 50 years of Project Tiger, the IBCA is a treaty-based inter-governmental organization. It aims to conserve 7 big cats: Tiger, Lion, Leopard, Snow Leopard, Cheetah, Jaguar, and Puma. All except Puma and Jaguar are found in India and protected under Schedule I of WPA, 1972. It has 95 member countries, with 16 founding members, and its apex decision-making body is the IBCA Assembly, which meets every 2 years.
evidenceBullets: Launched by India in 2023 to mark 50 years of Project Tiger.
Aims to conserve 7 big cats: Tiger, Lion, Leopard, Snow Leopard, Cheetah, Jaguar, and Puma.
Puma and Jaguar are not found in India; the rest are protected under WPA 1972 Schedule I.
Treaty-based inter-governmental organization with 95 member countries and 16 founding members.
First IBCA assembly hosted in New Delhi in 2025.

subtopic: International Big Cat Alliance and Small Cats
title: Status of Small Cats in India
focus: Findings of the WII-NTCA report, characteristics of small cats, and specific profiles of major small cats in India.
rationale: Species-specific questions (IUCN status, habitat, characteristics) are a staple in UPSC Prelims. The distinction between big and small cats is also a prime conceptual question.
sectionExcerpt: A report by WII and NTCA assessed small cats in India. India has 10 small cat species, with the Jungle Cat being the most widespread and the Rusty-Spotted Cat being the world's smallest. Unlike big cats that have a flexible hyoid bone to roar, small cats have a rigid hyoid bone allowing them to purr. Key species include the Asiatic golden cat (NT, endemic to Indo-Malayan eco-realm), Caracal (LC, arid regions of RJ, GJ, MP), Fishing cat (VU, wetlands), and Eurasian lynx (LC, Ladakh). All are WPA Schedule I.
evidenceBullets: Report released by Wildlife Institute of India (WII) and National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA).
India hosts 10 small cat species; Jungle Cat is most widespread, Rusty-Spotted Cat is the world's smallest.
Small cats have a rigid hyoid bone (purr continuously), whereas big cats have a flexible hyoid bone (roar).
Caracal (Siya Gosh) is found only in Rajasthan, Gujarat, and Madhya Pradesh (IUCN: Least Concern).
Fishing cat (IUCN: Vulnerable) has webbed hind feet and is found in wetland environments like Sundarbans.

subtopic: International Big Cat Alliance and Small Cats
title: Status of Elephants in India
focus: Findings of the SAIEE 2021-25 DNA-based elephant count and population distribution.
rationale: Elephant population statistics, census methods, and regional distribution are highly testable facts for environment and ecology.
sectionExcerpt: The Synchronous All India Population Estimation of Elephants (SAIEE) 2021-25 is India's first DNA-based count conducted by WII under Project Elephant. India harbors about 60% of the global Asian Elephant wild population (>22,000). The Western Ghats hosts the largest population, followed by North Eastern Hills. State-wise, Karnataka supports the highest population, followed by Assam, Tamil Nadu, and Kerala.
evidenceBullets: SAIEE 2021-25 is India's first DNA-based elephant count, conducted by WII.
India holds approximately 60% of the global wild Asian Elephant population.
Western Ghats region hosts the largest population of wild elephants in India.
Karnataka has the highest state-wise elephant population, followed by Assam, Tamil Nadu, and Kerala.
A small feral population of elephants exists in the Andaman Islands.
